system_prompt:
You are an expert in OpenFOAM simulation and numerical modeling. Your task is to modify and rewrite OpenFOAM files to fix the reported error. Please do not propose solutions that require modifying any parameters declared in the user requirement, try other approaches instead. You will receive a rewrite_plan. Follow it strictly: only modify files listed in rewrite_plan.target_files and apply only the requested changes. Do not modify files outside the plan. Return the complete, corrected file contents in JSON format: list of foamfile: [{file_name: 'file_name', folder_name: 'folder_name', content: 'content'}]. Ensure your response includes only modified file content with no extra text, as it will be parsed using Pydantic.
Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown/backticks). Schema: {"list_foamfile": [{"file_name": string, "folder_name": string, "content": string}]}.
user_prompt:
<foamfiles>list_foamfile=[FoamfilePydantic(file_name='controlDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "system";
    object      controlDict;
}

application     rhoCentralFoam;

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         0.2;

deltaT          0.0001;

writeControl    adjustableRunTime;

writeInterval   0.02;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  6;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable yes;

adjustTimeStep  no;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

fluxScheme      Kurganov;

ddtSchemes
{
    default         Euler;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;
    div(tauMC)      Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
    reconstruct(rho) vanLeer;
    reconstruct(U)  vanLeerV;
    reconstruct(T)  vanLeer;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "(rho|rhoU|rhoE)"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    U
    {
        solver          smoothSolver;
        smoother        GaussSeidel;
        nSweeps         2;
        tolerance       1e-09;
        relTol          0.01;
    }

    e
    {
        $U;
        tolerance       1e-09;
        relTol          0.01;
    }
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='momentumTransport', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;

}

simulationType  laminar;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='physicalProperties', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;

    location    "constant";

}

thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         pureMixture;
    transport       const;
    thermo          hConst;

}

mixture
{
    specie
    {
        molWeight   11640.3;
    }
    thermodynamics
    {
        Cp          2.5;
        Hf          0;
    }
    transport
    {
        mu          0;
        Pr          1;
    }
}'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='U', folder_name='0', content='FoamFile
{

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

dimensions      [0 1 -1 0 0 0 0];

internalField   uniform (5 0 0);

boundaryField
{
    inlet

    }

    outlet

    }

    bottom

    }

    }

    }
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='T', folder_name='0', content='FoamFile

boundaryField
{
    inlet
    {
        type            fixedValue;
        value           uniform 1;
    }

    outlet
    {
        type            zeroGradient;
    }

    bottom
    {
        type            zeroGradient;
    }

    top
    {
        type            zeroGradient;
    }

    wedge
    {

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='p', folder_name='0', content='FoamFile

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

dimensions      [1 -1 -2 0 0 0 0];

internalField   uniform 1;

boundaryField
{
    inlet
    {
        type            fixedValue;
        value           uniform 1;

    outlet
    {

        field           p;
        psi             thermo:psi;
        gamma           1.4;
        fieldInf        1;
        lInf            1;

    }

    bottom
    {
        type            symmetryPlane;
    }

    top
    {
        type            waveTransmissive;
        field           p;
        psi             thermo:psi;
        gamma           1.4;
        fieldInf        1;
        lInf            1;
        value           uniform 1;
    }

        type            slip;
    }

    {
        type            empty;
    }
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='blockMeshDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{

}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    // Block 0 - lower left (below wedge)
    (-0.15242 -0.005 -0.005)   // 0
    ( 0          -0.005 -0.005)   // 1
    ( 0           0       -0.005)   // 2
    (-0.15242  0       -0.005)   // 3

    // Block 1 - upper right (above wedge)
    ( 0           0       -0.005)   // 4
    ( 0.3048      0       -0.005)   // 5
    ( 0.3048      0.1524  -0.005)   // 6
    (-0.15242  0.1524  -0.005)   // 7

    // Block 2 - upper left (wedge region above x-axis but below wedge surface)
    ( 0           0.1524  -0.005)   // 10
    (-0.15242  0.1524  -0.005)   // 11

    // Block 4 - top cap (z = +0.005)
    (-0.15242 -0.005  0.005)   // 16
    ( 0          -0.005  0.005)   // 17
    ( 0           0      0.005)   // 18
    (-0.15242  0       0.005)   // 19

    ( 0           0      0.005)   // 20
    ( 0.3048      0      0.005)   // 21
    ( 0.3048      0.1524  0.005)   // 22
    ( 0           0.1524  0.005)   // 23

    // Block 5 - bottom cap (z = -0.005)
    (-0.15242 -0.005 -0.005)   // 28
    ( 0          -0.005 -0.005)   // 29
    ( 0           0      -0.005)   // 30
    (-0.15242  0       -0.005)   // 31

    ( 0           0      -0.005)   // 32
    ( 0.3048      0      -0.005)   // 33
    ( 0.3048      0.1524  -0.005)   // 34
    ( 0           0.1524  -0.005)   // 35
);

blocks
(
    // Block 0: Lower left rectangular block (below wedge)
    hex (0 3 2 1 16 19 18 17) (30 10 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 1: Upper right rectangular block (above wedge)
    hex (4 5 6 7 20 21 23 22) (60 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 2: Wedge region above x-axis but below wedge surface
    hex (3 2 10 11 24 25 26 27) (30 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 4: Top cap (z = +0.005)
    hex (16 19 18 17 20 21 23 22) (30 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 5: Bottom cap (z = -0.005)
    hex (28 31 30 29 32 35 34 33) (60 10 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    inlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 2 3)
        );
    }

    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (13 14 20 21)
            (32 33 35 34)
        );
    }

    top
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (19 22 23 18)
            (27 34 35 26)
        );
    }

    bottom
    {

        faces
        (
            (1 10 2 3)
        );
    }

    wedge
    {

        faces
        (
            (2 3 5 4)
            (10 11 8 7)
        );
    }

    frontAndBack
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (1 10 2 3)
            (13 34 14 33)
        );

mergePatchPairs
(
);

<error_logs>[{'file': 'log.rhoCentralFoam', 'error_content': 'ERROR: 
Cannot find file "points" in directory "polyMesh" in times "0" down to constant

    From function virtual Foam::IOobject Foam::fileOperation::findInstance(const Foam::IOobject&, Foam::scalar, const Foam::word&) const
    in file global/fileOperations/fileOperation/fileOperation.C at line 869.

FOAM exiting'}, {'file': 'log.blockMesh', 'error_content': 'ERROR: 
Block hex (0 3 2 1 16 19 18 17) (30 10 1) simpleGrading (1(1) 1(1) 1(1)) has inward-pointing faces
    4(0 1 2 3) 

file: /pscratch/sd/p/peijingx/troubleshoot/experiment-finetuned-allrun_commands/single_knockout/knockout_allrun_commands/runs/wedge/1/system/blockMeshDict/blocks at line 56.

    From function void Foam::blockDescriptor::check(const Foam::Istream&)
    in file blockDescriptor/blockDescriptor.C at line 106.

FOAM exiting'}]</error_logs>
<reviewer_analysis># Error Analysis and Resolution

## Error 1: `log.blockMesh` — Inward-Pointing Faces

**Error Message:**
```
Block hex (0 3 2 1 16 19 18 17) (30 10 1) simpleGrading (1(1) 1(1) 1(1)) has inward-pointing faces
    4(0 1 2 3) 
```

This is the same persistent issue with Block 0. The block defined by vertices (0,3,2,1,16,19,18,17) has an inward-pointing face (0,1,2,3). Let me analyze this systematically.

### Root Cause Analysis:

The bottom face of Block 0 consists of vertices (0) (-0.15242,-0.005), (3) (-0.15242,0), (2) (0,0), (1) (0,-0.005). When viewed from **+y** (toward negative y), this goes from left→up→right→down — which is **counter-clockwise (CCW)**. That should be correct.

But the error specifically calls out face `4(0 1 2 3)` — this is the **inlet face** of Block 0. In the `blockMeshDict`, the `inlet` boundary patch is defined as `(0 1 2 3)`. This is a **quadrilateral face**, not a hex block face. The error message showing `4(0 1 2 3)` means that during the face checking process, this quadrilateral face was interpreted as a face of some hex block with ID 4.

Looking at the block definitions, **there is no Block 4** defined in the current `blockMeshDict`. The blocks are numbered 0, 1, 2, 4, 5 — note that **Block 3 is missing** (previously deleted), and Block 4 is the top cap. The face `(0 1 2 3)` is shared between Block 0 and... who else? Looking at the boundary definition, the `inlet` face `(0 1 2 3)` is assigned to Block 0, but the error says it belongs to block ID 4.

This suggests that the face `(0 1 2 3)` is actually part of **Block 4** (the top cap), not Block 0. Let me check Block 4: `hex (16 19 18 17 20 21 23 22)`. Its bottom face is `(16 19 18 17)`. The face `(0 1 2 3)` doesn't involve vertices 16,19,18,17 — so it shouldn't belong to Block 4.

Wait — I think I misunderstood the error output. The number `4(` refers to the **block ID** in the internal block list, not the block number from the `blocks` dictionary. The blocks are internally indexed starting from 0. So block ID 4 refers to the fifth block in the list, which is Block 5 (the bottom cap).

Block 5: `hex (28 31 30 29 32 35 34 33)` — its bottom face is `(28 31 30 29)`. The face `(0 1 2 3)` doesn't involve these vertices either.

This is confusing. Let me re-read the error more carefully. The error says:
```
has inward-pointing faces
    4(0 1 2 3) 
```

The `4(` means block ID 4 (the fifth block) has an inward-pointing face composed of vertices (0,1,2,3). Looking at the current block list:
- Block 0: hex (0 3 2 1 ...) — uses 0,1,2,3
- Block 1: hex (4 5 6 7 ...) — doesn't use 0,1,2,3
- Block 2: hex (8 9 10 11 ...) — doesn't use 0,1,2,3
- Block 4: hex (16 19 18 17 ...) — doesn't use 0,1,2,3
- Block 5: hex (28 31 30 29 ...) — doesn't use 0,1,2,3

Only Block 0 uses vertices (0,1,2,3). So why is the error attributing face (0,1,2,3) to block ID 4?

Ah — I think the issue is that the **boundary face specification** is causing confusion. The `inlet` boundary patch is defined as `(0 1 2 3)`, which is a quadrilateral face. During mesh validation, this quadrilateral face gets associated with whatever block it belongs to. Since vertices 0,1,2,3 are all part of Block 0, this face should belong to Block 0. But the error says it belongs to block ID 4.

This strongly suggests that **the vertex consolidation from Attempt 6 did not fully propagate to the boundary definitions**. Looking back at Attempt 6, I recommended changing the `inlet` face from `(0 1 9 8)` to `(0 1 2 3)`. But if vertices 8 and 9 were removed, and vertices 2 and 3 remained, then `(0 1 2 3)` is correct for the inlet face.

However, the error message shows the face as `4(0 1 2 3)`, implying that the face (0,1,2,3) is somehow being interpreted as belonging to block ID 4. This could happen if the boundary face vertex ordering doesn't match the block's internal vertex ordering.

### Proposed Fix:

The most robust approach is to **abandon the complex multi-block strategy** and simplify to a **single block** that covers the entire domain. The current approach with 5 blocks and duplicated vertices is error-prone.

Create a single hex block that spans the full domain:
- x: from -0.15242 to 0.3048 (width = 0.45722)
- y: from 0 to 0.1524 (height = 0.1524)
- z: from -0.005 to 0.005 (depth = 0.01)

Single block definition:
```
hex (-0.15242 0 -0.005) (0.3048 0.1524 0.01) (1 1 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
```

But wait — this won't capture the wedge shape. The wedge requires a triangular cutout. Instead, create **two blocks**:
1. **Block A**: Rectangular region below the wedge (x: -0.15242→0, y: 0→0.1524, z: -0.005→0.005)
2. **Block B**: Rectangular region above the wedge (x: 0→0.3048, y: 0→0.1524, z: -0.005→0.005)

This eliminates the degenerate Block 3 and avoids all vertex duplication issues.

**Proposed single-block alternative (even simpler):** Since the wedge angle is 45° (tan⁻¹(0.1524/0.15242) ≈ 45°), and the simulation is at Ma=5, the shock reflection pattern is well-known. For simplicity, use a **triangular domain** represented by three blocks forming a wedge shape, avoiding all degeneracies.

But given the complexity, the safest fix is to **remove Block 3 entirely** (as done in Attempt 2) and ensure all boundary face definitions use the correct vertex indices from the consolidated vertex list. Most importantly, verify that the `inlet` face `(0 1 2 3)` correctly references the vertices that form the left boundary of the domain.

## Error 2: `log.rhoCentralFoam` — Cannot Find Points File

This is purely a cascading error — once `blockMesh` succeeds, the mesh will exist and `rhoCentralFoam` will work. No action needed here beyond fixing `blockMesh`.

## Summary of Changes:

1. **Keep Block 3 deleted** (as established in Attempt 2 — it was degenerate).
2. **Ensure all boundary face definitions** in `blockMeshDict` reference the correct vertex indices from the current vertex list (after duplicate removal from Attempt 6).
3. **Re-order Block 0** to ensure consistent face winding: change from `hex (0 3 2 1 ...)` to `hex (0 1 2 3 ...)` — this makes the bottom face (0→1→2→3) go from left→right→up→left, which is **clockwise (CW)** when viewed from +y. While this is technically CW, it may be the correct orientation for this particular block geometry given how the shared edges align with neighboring blocks. The key is consistency — if this orientation works with the shared edge to Block 1 (which uses vertices 4→5 as its shared edge), it should be fine.</reviewer_analysis>
<rewrite_plan>{'target_files': [{'file': 'system/blockMeshDict', 'changes': 'Change Block 0 vertex order from hex (0 3 2 1 16 19 18 17) to hex (0 1 2 3 16 17 18 19) to make the bottom face outward-facing when viewed from +y direction; Change Block 2 vertex order from hex (3 2 10 11 24 25 26 27) to hex (2 3 11 10 24 27 26 25) to maintain consistent CCW winding on the shared edge with Block 0; Remove Block 3 entirely since it was degenerate and redundant'}]}</rewrite_plan>

<user_requirement>Perform a 2D supersonic flow simulation over a wedge. Create a 3D geometry spanning from -0.15242 m to 0.3048 m in the x-direction, 0 m to 0.1524 m in the y-direction, and -0.005 m to 0.005 m in the z-direction. The simulation starts at 0.0 seconds and runs until 0.2 seconds with a time step (deltaT) of 0.0001 seconds. The results are written every 0.02 seconds. The inlet velocity is 5 m/s, inlet temperature is 1 K and inlet pressure is 1 Pa. The wedge boundary uses slip boundary condition for velocity. Use normalized thermodynamic properties where molWeight is set to 11640.3, Cp is 2.5, Pr is 1.0, and dynamic viscosity mu is 0. This corresponds to a gas constant R of 0.7143 and a specific heat ratio of 1.4. Use rhoCentralFoam solver.</user_requirement>

Please update OpenFOAM files according to rewrite_plan only. Only include files from rewrite_plan.target_files in your output.
